title: Making silage on a small scale
subtitle: Cutting, wilting, and ensiling grass for winter feed without a contractor.
excerpt: Small-scale silage, cut from one or two paddocks and sealed in round bales or a small clamp, is within reach of a holding with a tractor or access to hired machinery. The principle is straightforward: cut grass while the sugars are high, remove as much air as possible, and let the natural fermentation seal the bale.
sourceNotes: AHDB Grass and Forage Management Guide, ahdb.org.uk. SAC Consulting silage making guidance. Seymour, J., The New Complete Book of Self-Sufficiency (1976).

glossaryTerms.definition: Fermented grass or other forage preserved in anaerobic conditions. The natural fermentation produces lactic acid, which reduces pH and inhibits spoilage organisms.

The percentage of feed that is not water. Good silage for ruminants has a dry matter of 25 to 35 percent (clamp silage) or 35 to 45 percent (big bale silage). Too wet and it is acidic and unpalatable; too dry and it ferments poorly.
The period between cutting and ensiling when grass is left in the field to lose moisture. Wilting raises dry matter content and improves silage fermentation quality.

content.text: stores summer grass for winter without the quality losses that wet hay-making weather causes. If you have one or two paddocks and can use the feed, small-scale silage production is worth considering.

Cut at early head emergence, when around 10 percent of the sward is coming into ear. At this stage the grass has high sugar content for fermentation and high digestibility for the animals that will eat it. Cutting too late (full ear) reduces digestibility; cutting too early means the yield is low and the water content is very high, both of which cause problems in the clamp or bale.
for twenty-four to forty-eight hours after cutting raises the

content toward the target range and improves fermentation quality. Turn the swath once with a tedder or by hand to speed drying. Do not wilt for more than sixty hours in good weather, the grass starts to lose sugars needed for fermentation and risks bleaching if rained on then dried again.

A small clamp can be made in a clean bay or against a concrete wall. Pack the wilted grass as tightly as possible in layers, walking over each layer to expel air. Seal with heavy-duty black plastic sheeting overlapped at all joints, weighted down with old tyres or earth at the edges. Air is the enemy, any pocket of air leads to mould and spoilage.

Silage effluent is highly polluting to watercourses. A clamp producing effluent requires a collection sump or drainage point that directs the liquid away from ditches, streams, and drains. Plan the drainage before you fill the clamp; it is much harder to retrofit. Silage effluent in a stream is a serious environmental offence.

For a very small holding, hiring a contractor to bale and wrap the wilted grass is often more practical than building a clamp. Round bales wrapped with six layers of specialist silage wrap will keep for eighteen months to two years if stored off the ground and not damaged.

Any hole in the wrap must be repaired with silage tape immediately. A pin-hole no bigger than a matchhead allows air to enter and creates a spoilage zone that expands through the bale. Check all bales monthly through winter and repair any damage.
